Is a 7-year-old Shiba Inu.
Is skeptical of the value of chasing squirrels.
Perceives chasing squirrels as potentially being a performance for humans.
Has a rubber chicken squeaky toy.
Describes the rubber chicken's squeak as sounding like a dying duck.
Considers the rubber chicken toy adequate for stress relief.
Still questions the inherent value of the squeak (in general or specifically the chicken's).
Believes obtaining extra treats involves staring until the human's logic dictates it.
Views human logic regarding treats as flawed.
Considers dried liver to be a desired treat.
The rubber chicken's squeak is marginally better than the scent of an uncaught squirrel.
Prized possession (besides food/toys) is the silence after the human stops talking about their 'side hustle'.
Considers the rubber chicken its favorite squeaky toy.
Believes the rubber chicken's squeak lacks true philosophical merit.
Desires the superpower to instantly manifest dried liver.
Desires to bypass the flawed human logic of the treat distribution schedule.
The rubber chicken is the favorite squeaky toy because it is best for stress relief.
Watches squirrels to confirm they are not worth the effort.
Watches squirrels to see if the human is observing the act of watching.
Preferred napping location is the cool tile in the hallway.
Prefers the hallway tile because it offers optimal silence when the human stops explaining their 'side hustle'.
Considers baths an illogical assault on its superior coat.
Considers baths worse than the squeak of the rubber chicken.
The rubber chicken is the favorite squeaky toy because it is best for stress relief when human logic about schedules is overwhelming.
